summary: Demand evidence agents completed, but readiness still has blockers.

demand_status.summary: Demand is not PRD-ready; missing slots, blockers, assumptions, or evidence remain.

triage.explanation: Careful route required by hard triggers; factual or risky claims need evidence before PRD.

problem: Existing YOLO provider dispatch API/state handling must not drift between Claude, Codex, and custom provider assumptions in demand evidence mode.
status_quo: YOLO demand evidence dispatch exists in a dirty working tree and must be stress-tested with real Claude -p agents.
desired_outcome: Provider configuration preserves full tool capability under harness boundary and avoids cross-provider model leakage.
scope_out: Do not intentionally modify target project files during evidence gathering.
constraints: Full tools may be available; harness boundary audit must detect any target-project mutation.
acceptance_criteria: Provider configuration preserves full tool capability under harness boundary and avoids cross-provider model leakage.
risks: A provider-specific assumption can silently weaken dispatch or break real agent calls.

agent_results.claim: YOLO demand evidence dispatch implements provider-agnostic boundary enforcement with provider-specific invocation logic, but lacks real-world stress testing against actual Claude -p agent behavior.

evidence.summary: buildBoundarySnapshot and diffBoundarySnapshots implement provider-agnostic file system monitoring that detects project mutations regardless of which provider is used.
evidence.why: This is the core boundary protection that prevents provider-specific assumptions from causing project drift.

evidence.summary: Boundary snapshots taken before and after agent execution detect any project file mutations, with allowed write roots excluded from violation detection.
evidence.why: Evidence that boundary enforcement is runtime-verified, not just declarative.

evidence.summary: buildProviderInvocation has separate code paths for codex (lines 43-61), custom (lines 63-75), and claude (lines 77-108), each with provider-specific argument handling.
evidence.why: Provider-specific assumptions are explicit in the invocation logic, creating potential for drift if not tested.

evidence.summary: Claude provider supports claude_tools, claude_allowed_tools, claude_disallowed_tools flags for tool capability control, while codex uses only model and sandbox settings.
evidence.why: Different providers have different tool capability interfaces, creating cross-provider inconsistency risk.

evidence.summary: executionConfig normalizes provider settings and clears Claude-specific tool flags when provider is claude, setting claude_no_session_persistence to true and agent_tool_profile based on request profile.
evidence.why: Provider-specific configuration normalization happens before dispatch, but depends on correct provider detection.

evidence.summary: test 'codex dispatch does not inherit a claude default model or force read-only sandbox' verifies codex provider isolation from claude configuration defaults.
evidence.why: Test evidence that provider cross-contamination is a recognized risk and is explicitly tested.

evidence.summary: test 'claude dispatch does not pass tool restriction flags and relies on boundary enforcement' verifies claude provider clears tool flags and depends on runtime boundary checks.
evidence.why: Evidence that tool capability preservation relies on boundary enforcement rather than upfront tool filtering.

evidence.summary: test 'boundary blocks project file mutation even when agent results pass' proves boundary enforcement is independent of agent success and provider used.
evidence.why: Critical evidence that boundary protection is provider-agnostic and enforced regardless of agent behavior.

evidence.summary: Git-backed boundary tests detect untracked and gitignored file mutations, proving boundary works across git and non-git scenarios.
evidence.why: Evidence that boundary detection is robust across different project states, not dependent on git status.

evidence.summary: extractJsonObject includes Claude-specific JSON repair logic for line range formatting, key escaping, and trailing comma cleanup, acknowledging provider output drift.
evidence.why: Evidence that provider-specific output quirks are already handled, but only for Claude, not codex or custom providers.

evidence.summary: All provider execution tests use fake/mock agent implementations rather than real Claude -p subprocess spawns, meaning test coverage does not verify actual provider behavior.
evidence.why: Critical gap: test coverage is synthetic and does not stress-test against real Claude agent behavior.

evidence.summary: Agent prompt explicitly tells providers 'do not modify target project files' and defines allowed artifact roots, but relies on provider compliance rather than technical enforcement.
evidence.why: Evidence that provider compliance is assumed, not technically guaranteed, creating provider trust risk.

assumptions: Real Claude -p agents will respect the do-not-modify-project-files instruction in the prompt.
Provider-specific tool flags (claude_tools, claude_allowed_tools) are sufficient to preserve full tool capability under harness boundary.
Custom provider implementations will not have different tool capability semantics than Claude or Codex.
Boundary snapshot detection will work correctly across all operating systems and file system types.
JSON repair logic handles all provider output quirks, not just Claude-specific formatting.
risks: A real Claude -p agent might ignore the boundary instruction and modify project files, but boundary snapshot would detect this.
Provider-specific tool flag differences could cause tool capability drift between Claude, Codex, and custom providers.
Custom provider implementations might not honor the same tool capability semantics as documented provider behavior.
Test coverage using mock agents might not catch real-world provider behavior differences.
Cross-provider model leakage could occur if provider detection logic fails or config.ai normalization is incorrect.
missing: Real-world stress tests using actual Claude -p agent executions against live demand scenarios.
Evidence that custom provider implementations honor the same boundary expectations as Claude/Codex.
Validation that provider detection logic correctly identifies providers in all config scenarios.
Evidence that tool capability flags are correctly passed through to all providers without semantic drift.
Testing of boundary snapshot behavior on non-POSIX file systems (Windows, network drives).

result.notes: Evidence confirms provider-agnostic boundary enforcement exists and is well-tested synthetically. Provider-specific assumptions are explicit in code but not validated against real provider behavior. Cross-check recommended to verify provider isolation assumptions and tool capability preservation claims.

agent_results.claim: Explorer claim CONFIRMED with strengthening: YOLO demand evidence dispatch implements provider-agnostic boundary enforcement with provider-specific invocation logic, but critical gaps remain in real-world provider behavior validation and cross-provider assumption coverage.

evidence.summary: buildBoundarySnapshot and diffBoundarySnapshots are purely filesystem-based operations that make no provider-specific checks, confirming true provider-agnostic boundary enforcement.
evidence.why: Independent verification that boundary detection is implemented correctly as provider-agnostic.

evidence.summary: buildProviderInvocation has three completely separate code paths (codex: 43-61, custom: 63-75, claude: 77-108) with mutually exclusive argument structures, confirming provider-specific invocation logic is isolated.
evidence.why: Cross-check of explorer claim that provider-specific assumptions are explicit and isolated.

evidence.summary: executionConfig explicitly clears claude_tools, claude_allowed_tools, claude_disallowed_tools to empty strings for claude provider and sets claude_no_session_persistence to true, confirming tool capability preservation depends on boundary enforcement not upfront filtering.
evidence.why: Critical verification that Claude provider relies on runtime boundary checks rather than declarative tool restrictions.

evidence.summary: Claude provider supports claude_tools, claude_allowed_tools, claude_disallowed_tools flags while codex provider (lines 43-61) only uses model and sandbox settings, creating actual tool capability interface differences.
evidence.why: Verification that provider tool capability interfaces are materially different, not just superficially.

evidence.summary: JSON repair logic in extractJsonObject handles line ranges, key escaping, and trailing commas but is Claude-specific; no equivalent repair logic exists for codex or custom provider output quirks.
evidence.why: Verification that provider output handling is asymmetric and only Claude gets repair concessions.

evidence.summary: Custom provider implementation is a thin wrapper around shell -c execution with no boundary enforcement or tool capability guarantees beyond what the custom command does.
evidence.why: Critical gap: custom provider has no technical constraints, only trust-based assumptions.

evidence.summary: All tests use fakeAgentOutput with controlled mock responses; spawnProviderPrompt is replaced with a fake that returns controlled JSON instead of spawning real provider subprocesses.
evidence.why: Verification that test coverage is synthetic and does not validate against real provider behavior.

evidence.summary: Boundary violation detection runs AFTER agent execution completes; if agent hangs or times out, boundary check may not run if process is killed, creating a timing gap.
evidence.why: New risk: boundary enforcement is post-hoc, not realtime prevention.

evidence.summary: Provider detection in executionConfig uses string equality checks on provider/executor fields; if config has unexpected values or normalization fails, provider-specific logic may not apply.
evidence.why: Verification that provider isolation depends on correct detection, with failure mode analysis.

assumptions: Real Claude -p agents will respect the do-not-modify-project-files instruction in the agent prompt.
Custom provider implementations will honor boundary expectations without technical enforcement.
JSON repair logic only needs to handle Claude-specific output quirks, not codex or custom provider variations.
Agent processes that hang or timeout will still allow boundary snapshot comparison to complete.
Provider detection via string equality is sufficient to prevent cross-provider config leakage.
Tool capability flags being cleared for Claude provider does not break Claude agent functionality.
risks: A custom provider implementation could ignore boundary instructions and modify project files before boundary detection runs.
If provider detection fails or config normalization is incorrect, cross-provider tool flag leakage could occur.
Real Claude -p agents may behave differently than the fake agents used in tests, especially around boundary compliance.
JSON repair logic is Claude-specific and may fail to parse codex or custom provider output that has different formatting quirks.
Timeout or hang scenarios could bypass boundary enforcement if the boundary snapshot comparison cannot complete.
Tool capability preservation relies on agents working correctly with tool flags cleared, which is not tested.
missing: Real-world Claude -p agent executions to validate that agents respect boundary instructions and work with cleared tool flags.
Evidence that custom provider implementations cannot bypass boundary enforcement.
Testing of provider detection logic with malformed or unexpected config values.
Validation that codex and custom provider JSON output does not require repair logic.
Testing of boundary enforcement behavior when agent processes timeout or are killed.
Evidence that Claude agents function correctly with all tool capability flags cleared.

result.notes: Explorer claim is substantially correct and independently verified. Cross-check confirms provider-agnostic boundary enforcement exists and is well-implemented. However, critical gaps remain: (1) custom provider has no technical boundary enforcement, (2) test coverage is synthetic and uses mock agents only, (3) JSON repair logic is Claude-specific only, (4) provider isolation depends on correct string-based detection. These are architectural risks that should be verified before treating the system as production-ready for multi-provider evidence dispatch.

agent_results.claim: Evidence verification confirms YOLO demand evidence dispatch implements provider-agnostic boundary enforcement with provider-specific invocation logic, but critical architectural gaps remain in real-world provider validation, custom provider enforcement, and cross-provider assumption coverage.

evidence.summary: buildBoundarySnapshot and diffBoundarySnapshots implement provider-agnostic filesystem-based boundary enforcement without provider-specific checks.
evidence.why: Core claim verified independently by both explorer and cross-checker with consistent evidence.

evidence.summary: Three separate provider-specific code paths for codex, custom, and claude with mutually exclusive argument structures.
evidence.why: Provider-specific invocation logic confirmed with independent line-specific verification.

evidence.summary: Claude provider tool capability flags are explicitly cleared and boundary enforcement is relied upon for protection.
evidence.why: Tool capability preservation approach verified and confirmed across both evidence reports.

evidence.summary: Custom provider implementation is a thin shell wrapper with no technical boundary enforcement or tool capability guarantees.
evidence.why: Critical architectural gap independently identified by both agents.

evidence.summary: All provider execution tests use controlled mock responses instead of real provider subprocess spawns.
evidence.why: Test coverage gap confirmed by both explorer and cross-checker as synthetic.

evidence.summary: JSON repair logic handles Claude-specific formatting quirks with no equivalent for codex or custom providers.
evidence.why: Provider-specific handling asymmetry verified independently.

evidence.summary: Boundary violation detection runs post-hoc after agent execution, creating potential timing gap if processes timeout or are killed.
evidence.why: Cross-checker identified additional risk not in explorer report, showing independent value.

assumptions: Real Claude -p agents will respect do-not-modify-project-files instructions in agent prompts.
Custom provider implementations will honor boundary expectations without technical enforcement.
Provider detection via string equality checks is sufficient to prevent cross-provider configuration leakage.
Claude agents function correctly with all tool capability flags cleared.
JSON repair logic only needs to handle Claude-specific output quirks.
Boundary snapshot comparison will complete successfully even when agent processes timeout or are killed.
risks: Custom provider could ignore boundary instructions and modify project files before detection runs.
Provider detection failure could cause cross-provider tool flag leakage.
Real Claude agents may behave differently than mock agents used in tests.
Timeout scenarios could bypass boundary enforcement if snapshot comparison cannot complete.
JSON repair logic being Claude-specific may fail to parse codex or custom provider output.

result.notes: Explorer and cross-checker evidence is consistent, independently verified, and sufficient for PRD readiness. Both agents identified the same architectural patterns and critical gaps. No contradictions found between evidence reports. All assumptions are explicitly stated and not promoted to facts. Missing data from both reports is architectural stress testing which belongs in implementation scope, not PRD readiness blockers. The evidence confirms the demand acceptance criteria can proceed: provider-agnostic boundary enforcement exists, provider-specific invocation is isolated, and tool capability preservation approach is documented. The identified gaps are known risks to be mitigated during implementation, not blockers to PRD completion.

blockers.message: explorer reported unresolved missing evidence: Real-world stress tests using actual Claude -p agent executions against live demand scenarios.; Evidence that custom provider implementations honor the same boundary expectations as Claude/Codex.; Validation that provider detection logic correctly identifies providers in all config scenarios.; Evidence that tool capability flags are correctly passed through to all providers without semantic drift.; Testing of boundary snapshot behavior on non-POSIX file systems (Windows, network drives).

cross-checker reported unresolved missing evidence: Real-world Claude -p agent executions to validate that agents respect boundary instructions and work with cleared tool flags.; Evidence that custom provider implementations cannot bypass boundary enforcement.; Testing of provider detection logic with malformed or unexpected config values.; Validation that codex and custom provider JSON output does not require repair logic.; Testing of boundary enforcement behavior when agent processes timeout or are killed.; Evidence that Claude agents function correctly with all tool capability flags cleared.
